skill.name: migrating-to-db
skill.description: Guides Logseq users through migrating Markdown graphs to the Logseq Database format, including backups, import settings, validation, and common fixes.
skill.summary: Plan and validate Logseq Markdown to Database migrations.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks as command execution, but the skill contains documentation examples and inline code notation, not executable automation. The only real external references are Logseq documentation and forum links, and no prompt injection or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Shell Backup Examples Are User Documentation
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged shell-like content, including a backup command block and many Markdown inline-code spans. The actual shell examples are limited to local backup commands in documentation, and the skill does not declare shell execution tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced documentation snippets or inline Markdown notation. No executable script file, shell tool allowance, or automated command invocation was found.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Logseq documentation and Logseq community discussion pages. They do not collect data, embed tracking parameters, or instruct the agent to transmit user content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible resource links at the end of the skill. No evidence found of hidden network calls or exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Keyword Detections Are Benign Context
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alert appears to match the term MD in a Markdown migration description, not a cryptographic algorithm. The system-reconnaissance alert appears on guidance to rename Logseq pages before migration, not host or network discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows both detections are ordinary prose about Logseq Markdown migration. No cryptographic operation or reconnaissance command is present at the cited lines.

content.value_statement: Logseq Markdown to Database migration can change tags, properties, namespaces, and queries. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan, validate, and troubleshoot the migration.
actual_capabilities: Explains the main differences between Logseq Markdown graphs and Database graphs.
Provides a pre-migration checklist for backups, pages, properties, tags, and namespaces.
Compares migration strategies for small graphs, large active graphs, and selective imports.
Suggests validation steps for page counts, orphaned blocks, properties, and broken references.
Documents common migration issues such as lost property types and query compatibility changes.
limitations: Does not perform the Logseq migration or modify graph files directly.
Reflects Logseq DB alpha-era behavior and may need confirmation against current Logseq releases.
Cannot guarantee complete plugin, whiteboard, sync, or export compatibility.
Requires users to verify backups and migration results in their own Logseq environment.

use_cases.title: Plan a Personal Graph Migration
use_cases.description: Create a checklist for moving a personal Markdown graph into Logseq DB with backups and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Assess Workspace Migration Risk
use_cases.description: Identify namespace, tag, property, and query changes before migrating an active knowledge workspace.

use_cases.title: Repair Migration Issues
use_cases.description: Troubleshoot broken references, lost property types, and outdated Datalog queries after importing data.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Migration Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare my Logseq Markdown graph for DB migration. Focus on backups, tags, properties, namespaces, and validation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Migration Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare big bang, parallel operation, and selective migration for my Logseq graph. My graph size is [size] and my workflow is [workflow].

prompt_templates.title: Review Tag and Property Decisions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Logseq tags and properties before DB import: [list]. Recommend which tags should become classes and which properties need types.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Post-Migration Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: I migrated to Logseq DB and found these issues: [issues]. Help me prioritize checks for references, properties, namespaces, and queries.

output_examples.input: I want to migrate a small personal Logseq graph.
output_examples.output: A concise checklist covering backup creation, graph cleanup, import choices, page and block count checks, and a reminder to keep the Markdown graph.

output_examples.input: My migrated tags did not become useful classes.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting path that separates simple reference tags from class candidates, then recommends property definitions for the important classes.

output_examples.input: Old queries stopped working after migration.
output_examples.output: A compatibility review that explains changed attributes and suggests rewriting queries against the DB graph model.

best_practices: Create a complete backup before testing any migration.
Test with a small subset before migrating an active graph.
Validate pages, blocks, properties, references, and important queries after import.
anti_patterns: Deleting the Markdown graph immediately after a successful import.
Migrating without deciding which tags should become classes.
Assuming old queries and plugins will work without review.

faq.question: Does this skill migrate my graph automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, checklists, validation steps, and troubleshooting advice for a manual Logseq migration.

faq.question: Can it help me choose between migration strategies?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares full, parallel, and selective migration approaches based on graph size and workflow risk.

faq.question: Does it cover tag-to-class conversion?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when tags can remain references and when they may become classes with properties.

faq.question: Can it fix broken references?
faq.answer: It can guide diagnosis and repair steps, but it does not directly edit your Logseq graph.

faq.question: Is Logseq DB compatibility guaranteed?
faq.answer: No. Logseq DB behavior, sync, plugins, whiteboards, and export support may change across releases.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
